era_context: Continuation of Early Bronze Age III. Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin remains at or near peak power. Egyptian Old Kingdom (Dynasty 6) continues its decline under Pepi II. Indus Valley Civilisation maintains its mature urban and trade networks. In China, the traditional Xia Dynasty period continues (unconfirmed archaeologically).

events.title: Akkadian administrative consolidation under Naram-Sin
events.description: Naram-Sin continues to consolidate Akkadian control through military campaigns and administrative reorganisation. He establishes governors in conquered territories and maintains a standing army. Trade and tribute flow to the Akkadian heartland. However, evidence from later inscriptions and administrative records suggests growing pressure from the Gutians in the north and internal resistance from Sumerian city-states.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative tablets

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested. The specific events of -2518 are not individually documented; this entry describes the general trajectory of his reign during this period.

events.title: Indus Valley trade networks at peak activity
events.region: Indus Valley, Mesopotamia, Persian Gulf, Central Asia
events.description: Archaeological evidence suggests that Indus Valley trade with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf reaches its height during the mature Harappan period. Indus seals and weights are found in Mesopotamian contexts; Mesopotamian goods appear in Indus sites. This trade is facilitated by maritime routes and possibly overland routes through Balochistan and the Persian Gulf. The standardisation of Indus weights and measures suggests deliberate coordination of trade practices.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Indus sites (Harappa, Mohenjo-daro) and Mesopotamian sites (Ur, Lagash, Uruk)
Indus seals found in Mesopotamian contexts

events.certainty_note: The existence of Indus-Mesopotamian trade is archaeologically confirmed. The peak of this trade is estimated to fall within the mature Harappan period (c. 2600–1900 BCE), of which -2518 is part.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the attribution of individual years to specific administrative or military actions during Naram-Sin's reign is speculative; contemporary inscriptions do not provide year-by-year narratives.
historiographic_note: The year -2518 is not individually documented in any surviving source. The events described are inferred from the broader context of Naram-Sin's reign and the archaeological record of the Indus Valley Civilisation. Mesopotamian chronology carries significant uncertainty; the absolute dating of -2518 is approximate within ±50–100 years.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Naram-Sin's reign and consolidation.
